name: Hydrogen
fun_fact: Hydrogen is the lightest element and makes up most of the sun.
story_seed_hooks: {name} floats balloons up into the sky
stars are giant balls of glowing {name}
{name} is so light it tries to escape into space

name: Helium
fun_fact: Helium makes balloons float and makes voices sound funny when you breathe it in.
story_seed_hooks: {name} was first spotted in the sun before on Earth
breathing tiny bits of {name} makes voices squeaky

name: Lithium
fun_fact: Lithium is the lightest metal and powers many phones, tablets, and electric cars.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is so light it can float on water
phones run on tiny bits of {name}

name: Beryllium
fun_fact: Beryllium is a strong, light metal that helps build airplanes and spaceships.

name: Boron
fun_fact: Boron helps make strong glass that doesn't crack when it gets hot.

name: Carbon
fun_fact: Carbon is in every living thing on Earth, from trees to people to your pet dog.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is inside every plant and animal
diamonds and pencil tips are both made of {name}

name: Nitrogen
fun_fact: Most of the air we breathe is nitrogen, even more than oxygen.
story_seed_hooks: {name} makes up most of the air
{name} helps plants grow tall and green

name: Oxygen
fun_fact: Oxygen is the part of the air our lungs use to keep us alive.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is what our lungs love to breathe

name: Fluorine
fun_fact: Fluorine helps make toothpaste that keeps your teeth strong.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is in toothpaste for strong teeth

name: Neon
fun_fact: Neon glows bright orange-red when electricity flows through it, making colorful signs.
story_seed_hooks: {name} signs glow orange in the dark
{name} only glows when zapped with electricity

name: Sodium
fun_fact: Sodium combines with chlorine to make the salt you sprinkle on food.
story_seed_hooks: {name} burns with a bright yellow flame

name: Magnesium
fun_fact: Magnesium burns with a super-bright white flame, which is why old camera flashes used it.

name: Aluminum
fun_fact: Aluminum is the light, shiny metal in soda cans and kitchen foil.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is the metal in soda cans
{name} is light enough to fly in airplanes

name: Silicon
fun_fact: Silicon is the main ingredient in computer chips and most beach sand.

name: Phosphorus
fun_fact: Phosphorus is in the tip of every match and helps your bones stay strong.

name: Sulfur
fun_fact: Sulfur is the bright yellow stuff near volcanoes, and it smells like rotten eggs.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is the yellow dust near volcanoes

name: Chlorine
fun_fact: Chlorine keeps swimming pools clean and is the other half of table salt.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is half of every salt grain

name: Argon
fun_fact: Argon is a quiet gas that fills old light bulbs to keep them from burning out.

name: Potassium
fun_fact: Bananas are full of potassium, which helps your muscles work properly.

name: Calcium
fun_fact: Calcium builds strong bones and teeth and is in milk, cheese, and yogurt.

name: Scandium
fun_fact: Scandium makes baseball bats and bicycle frames super light and strong.

name: Titanium
fun_fact: Titanium is so strong and light that airplanes, ships, and even toothpaste use it.

name: Vanadium
fun_fact: Vanadium makes steel tools extra tough, like the wrenches in a car mechanic's shop.
story_seed_hooks: {name} was named after a Norse goddess
{name} can glow in five different colors

name: Chromium
fun_fact: Chromium makes the shiny silver coating on car bumpers and bicycle bells.

name: Manganese
fun_fact: Manganese helps batteries work and gives some glass a pretty purple color.
story_seed_hooks: {name} hides in the deep ocean floor

name: Iron
fun_fact: Iron makes your blood red and is the metal inside skyscrapers and bridges.
story_seed_hooks: bridges and buildings stand tall with {name}

name: Cobalt
fun_fact: Cobalt makes the deep blue color in old pottery and stained-glass windows.

name: Nickel
fun_fact: Nickel is the shiny metal in the five-cent coin called a nickel.

name: Copper
fun_fact: Copper turns green when it's outside for a long time, like the Statue of Liberty.
story_seed_hooks: {name} turned the Statue of Liberty green

name: Zinc
fun_fact: Zinc keeps cuts and scrapes from getting sore and also makes pennies feel light.

name: Gallium
fun_fact: Gallium melts in your hand because its melting point is below body temperature.

name: Germanium
fun_fact: Germanium was used in the very first transistors that started the computer age.

name: Arsenic
fun_fact: Arsenic is a metalloid that helps make computer chips and tiny LED lights.

name: Selenium
fun_fact: Selenium helps photocopiers see and copy pictures on paper.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is in some glass to color it red

name: Bromine
fun_fact: Bromine is one of only two liquid elements at room temperature, the other is mercury.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is a red liquid at room temperature
{name} was first found near the sea
{name} is one of only two liquid elements

name: Krypton
fun_fact: Krypton glows pale lavender in lights and shares its name with Superman's home planet.

name: Rubidium
fun_fact: Rubidium is named for its ruby-red color when it burns in a flame.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is soft enough to cut with a knife

name: Strontium
fun_fact: Strontium makes fireworks burn bright red, which is why holiday rockets are so colorful.
story_seed_hooks: {name} glows in some dim emergency signs

name: Yttrium
fun_fact: Yttrium helps make the bright red dots on old TV screens.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is named after a Swedish village

name: Zirconium
fun_fact: Zirconium makes fake diamonds that sparkle just like real ones in jewelry.

name: Niobium
fun_fact: Niobium helps make super-fast magnets that lift trains off the tracks.
story_seed_hooks: {name} lifts magnetic trains off the rails
{name} is named after a Greek princess

name: Molybdenum
fun_fact: Molybdenum makes steel tools tough enough to cut through other metals.

name: Technetium
fun_fact: Technetium was the first element ever made by people, in a lab in 1937.

name: Ruthenium
fun_fact: Ruthenium makes the tiny gold tip on fancy fountain pens last extra long.

name: Rhodium
fun_fact: Rhodium is one of the rarest metals and gives jewelry its bright silvery shine.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is one of the rarest metals on Earth

name: Palladium
fun_fact: Palladium cleans up car exhaust so the air stays cleaner to breathe.
story_seed_hooks: {name} can soak up lots of hydrogen

name: Silver
fun_fact: Silver is the shiniest metal of all and turns black when it sits in the air too long.

name: Cadmium
fun_fact: Cadmium paints used to make sunny yellow colors in old artists' paintings.

name: Indium
fun_fact: Indium makes touchscreens work so you can tap your tablet and phone.

name: Tin
fun_fact: Tin coats steel cans so the soup or beans inside don't rust the can.
story_seed_hooks: {name} coats food cans on the inside
{name} soldiers stood in old toy boxes

name: Antimony
fun_fact: Antimony helps make fire-resistant clothes that don't catch fire easily.
story_seed_hooks: {name} was used as eye makeup long ago

name: Tellurium
fun_fact: Tellurium helps solar panels turn sunshine into power for homes.

name: Iodine
fun_fact: Iodine is added to table salt to help your body grow strong.

name: Xenon
fun_fact: Xenon glows bright white in flash bulbs and powers ion engines on space probes.

name: Cesium
fun_fact: Cesium runs the most accurate clocks in the world, losing only a second every millions of years.
story_seed_hooks: {name} melts in a warm hand like butter

name: Barium
fun_fact: Barium makes fireworks burn bright green and helps doctors take pictures of stomachs.

name: Lanthanum
fun_fact: Lanthanum makes camera lenses sharper so pictures look crisp and clear.

name: Cerium
fun_fact: Cerium polishes glass to a mirror finish and is the most common rare-earth metal.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is named after a tiny planet

name: Praseodymium
fun_fact: Praseodymium colors special goggles green so welders can see safely through bright sparks.

name: Neodymium
fun_fact: Neodymium makes the strongest magnets on Earth, found in headphones and hard drives.

name: Promethium
fun_fact: Promethium is named after a hero from Greek myths who gave fire to people.
story_seed_hooks: {name} glows in some old watch faces
{name} is named after a Greek hero

name: Samarium
fun_fact: Samarium makes powerful magnets that still work even when very hot.

name: Europium
fun_fact: Europium makes the red color in TV screens and the glow in some banknotes.

name: Gadolinium
fun_fact: Gadolinium helps doctors take clearer pictures inside the body with MRI scans.

name: Terbium
fun_fact: Terbium makes the bright green color you see on TV and tablet screens.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is named after a Swedish village

name: Dysprosium
fun_fact: Dysprosium makes magnets that don't lose their pull even when very hot.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is hard to find on Earth

name: Holmium
fun_fact: Holmium creates the strongest magnetic fields of any natural element.

name: Erbium
fun_fact: Erbium colors glass and ceramics a pretty pink and helps light travel through cables.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is named after a Swedish village

name: Thulium
fun_fact: Thulium glows blue in some special lasers and was once the rarest of the rare earths.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is one of the rarest rare-earths
{name} was found by a Swedish scientist

name: Ytterbium
fun_fact: Ytterbium helps make atomic clocks that keep time more precisely than ever before.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is named after a Swedish village

name: Lutetium
fun_fact: Lutetium is one of the rarest and most expensive metals to dig out of the ground.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is one of the rarest metals

name: Hafnium
fun_fact: Hafnium makes super-fast computer chips run cooler and use less power.

name: Tantalum
fun_fact: Tantalum stores power inside phones, tablets, and game consoles in tiny capacitors.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is named after a Greek myth

name: Tungsten
fun_fact: Tungsten has the highest melting point of any metal, which is why it's in light-bulb wires.

name: Rhenium
fun_fact: Rhenium helps jet engines spin hot and fast without melting.
story_seed_hooks: {name} was the last natural element discovered
{name} is named after a German river

name: Osmium
fun_fact: Osmium is the densest natural element, so a small chunk feels surprisingly heavy.

name: Iridium
fun_fact: Iridium is so tough that scientists use it to mark the original meter standard.
story_seed_hooks: {name} fell from space in old meteors
{name} is one of the rarest metals on Earth

name: Platinum
fun_fact: Platinum is so rare that even less of it exists than gold, and it makes fancy wedding rings.

name: Gold
fun_fact: Gold is so soft you can hammer it into sheets thin enough to see through.
story_seed_hooks: {name} doesn't rust, so old crowns still shine
tiny flecks of {name} hide in rivers

name: Mercury
fun_fact: Mercury is the only metal that's liquid at room temperature, like silver water.

name: Thallium
fun_fact: Thallium is one of the few elements whose name comes from a Greek word for a green twig.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is named after a green plant
{name} burns with a bright green flame

name: Lead
fun_fact: Lead is one of the heaviest common metals and was used in pencils long ago.

name: Bismuth
fun_fact: Bismuth grows into beautiful rainbow staircase crystals that look like tiny castles.
story_seed_hooks: {name} shines pink and blue and gold

name: Polonium
fun_fact: Polonium was named after Poland by Marie Curie, who discovered it.

name: Astatine
fun_fact: Astatine is so rare that less than a teaspoonful exists on the whole planet.
story_seed_hooks: {name} was made in a lab in California

name: Radon
fun_fact: Radon is a gas that seeps up from the ground and is checked for in basements.

name: Francium
fun_fact: Francium is so rare that there are only a few atoms of it on Earth at any time.
story_seed_hooks: only tiny puffs of {name} are made at once

name: Radium
fun_fact: Radium glows in the dark and was once painted on old watch dials to read the time.

name: Actinium
fun_fact: Actinium glows a pretty pale blue in the dark and starts the actinide family of elements.
story_seed_hooks: {name} glows pale blue in the dark

name: Thorium
fun_fact: Thorium is named after Thor, the Norse god of thunder.

name: Protactinium
fun_fact: Protactinium's name means it comes before actinium when it breaks down.
story_seed_hooks: {name} was hard to find for many years

name: Uranium
fun_fact: Uranium is named after the planet Uranus and powers nuclear reactors that make electricity.

name: Neptunium
fun_fact: Neptunium is named after the planet Neptune, just past Uranus in our solar system.
story_seed_hooks: {name} comes after uranium on the table

name: Plutonium
fun_fact: Plutonium is named after the dwarf planet Pluto and powers some space probes.

name: Americium
fun_fact: Americium is in many home smoke detectors and helps keep families safe from fires.

name: Curium
fun_fact: Curium is named after Marie and Pierre Curie, two famous element-discovering scientists.

name: Berkelium
fun_fact: Berkelium is named after Berkeley, California, where scientists first made it.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is one of the heaviest synthetic elements

name: Californium
fun_fact: Californium helps find gold deep underground and start some nuclear reactors.

name: Einsteinium
fun_fact: Einsteinium is named after Albert Einstein, the famous scientist who imagined time bending.
story_seed_hooks: {name} was found in a hydrogen-bomb cloud

name: Fermium
fun_fact: Fermium is named after Enrico Fermi, a scientist who built the first nuclear reactor.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is made only in tiny puffs

name: Mendelevium
fun_fact: Mendelevium is named after the scientist who first organized the periodic table.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is made one atom at a time

name: Nobelium
fun_fact: Nobelium is named after Alfred Nobel, who started the Nobel Prizes.
story_seed_hooks: {name} is made one atom at a time

name: Lawrencium
fun_fact: Lawrencium is named after Ernest Lawrence, who invented a machine called a cyclotron.

name: Rutherfordium
fun_fact: Rutherfordium is named after Ernest Rutherford, who discovered the center of the atom.

name: Dubnium
fun_fact: Dubnium is named after Dubna, a Russian town where scientists made it.

name: Seaborgium
fun_fact: Seaborgium is named after Glenn Seaborg, who discovered many heavy elements.

name: Bohrium
fun_fact: Bohrium is named after Niels Bohr, who imagined how electrons orbit atoms.

name: Hassium
fun_fact: Hassium is named after the German state of Hesse, where scientists made it.

name: Meitnerium
fun_fact: Meitnerium is named after Lise Meitner, a scientist who studied splitting atoms.

name: Darmstadtium
fun_fact: Darmstadtium is named after Darmstadt, the German city where scientists made it.

name: Roentgenium
fun_fact: Roentgenium is named after Wilhelm Roentgen, who discovered X-rays.

name: Copernicium
fun_fact: Copernicium is named after Nicolaus Copernicus, who taught that Earth circles the sun.

name: Nihonium
fun_fact: Nihonium is named after Nihon, which is the Japanese word for Japan.

name: Flerovium
fun_fact: Flerovium is named after Georgy Flyorov, a Russian scientist who studied atoms.

name: Moscovium
fun_fact: Moscovium is named after Moscow, the capital city of Russia.

name: Livermorium
fun_fact: Livermorium is named after Livermore, a California lab where scientists studied it.

name: Tennessine
fun_fact: Tennessine is named after the U.S. state of Tennessee, where some of the work happened.

name: Oganesson
fun_fact: Oganesson is the heaviest element ever made and is named after Yuri Oganessian.
